data.title: My sister is buying our parents' $3 million house, but wants to deduct $100K for renovations. Who's right?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'If she didn't want the home, it would have been sold at fair market value'

"My siblings and I got tired of the whining and agreed that she would only have to pay the lesser of $3 million or the appraised price of the house." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

Several years ago, when my parents were updating their will and living trust, my sister expressed interest in eventually buying their home in Kona, Hawaii. Our parents asked all four kids if this would be OK and we agreed that it would be fine. Initially, we discussed that the price would be fair market value, but she claimed she would only be able to afford a maximum of $3 million and said that the house would still be in the family.

This is not the home we grew up in, and I'm not sure any of us could afford to visit more than once every five years. That said, my siblings and I got tired of the whining and agreed that she would only have to pay the lesser of $3 million or the appraised price of the house. Ultimately, the money from the sale of the house will go into mom's trust account and be used to help her until her final days. Any money left will be split equally among the four of us.

Five years have passed, and our father recently passed away. The will specifies that the home should be appraised within six months of the first parent's death and purchased within two years (for capital gains and step up/cost basis reasons). The appraisal came in at $3.2 million. Our mother is now in assisted living, and the house is being rented (to cover the cost of assisted living) while my sister prepares to sell her mainland house.

Don't miss: I'm 70, a widow and struggled financially. How do I let go of the resentment I feel towards my wealthy, unhelpful brother?

$100K maintenance costs

The issue is that before we could rent the house, some maintenance needed to be done. The pool needed to be resurfaced, a new barbecue added and some trees trimmed. That work totaled $100,000. I let my sister know that she will have to pay for this, as she would be getting the benefit of this work once she purchased the home. She did pay for it, but now says that she is only paying $2.9 million for the house.

In her mind, she paid in order to get the home rented to help support mom's assisted-living costs and would never have done it if she thought she couldn't deduct it from the cost of the house. The will is silent on maintenance issues, but in my mind, she will be getting $200,000 in equity immediately, only having to pay $3 million on a house valued at $3.2 million. If she didn't want the home, it would have been sold at fair market value.

She pointed out that if we sold it on the market we would have had to do the maintenance work, which is true, but in my opinion we would likely have sold it for over $3.3 million, coming out $200,000 ahead, accounting for the $100,000 renovation costs. Am I wrong thinking that she should cover the maintenance costs that will only benefit her and her family down the road? Is there another way that makes this fair to all of us?

Trying To Be Reasonable

Related: When my friend was on her deathbed, her parents took $25,000 in cash and won't give it to her son. Do we tell him?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

You agreed not to sell the home on the open market, so the "fair market value" was always going to be an issue of contention.

Dear Trying,

But you didn't sell it for $3.3 million.

You settled on a $3 million sale price under a family arrangement that was intentionally below the appraised value.

You can't have both realities coexisting. It will mess with everyone's heads. The point is that you agreed not to sell the home on the open market, so the "fair market value" was always going to be an issue of contention. Once you chose a private, discounted sale to a family member, normal market comparisons stopped being clean reference points. And you want your sister to pay for the $100,000 renovations to make up for future equity gains in the home. The crux of the issue is that she paid for work on a trust-owned asset before she legally owned it, which is not the same thing as a buyer upgrading their own home. If you were buying a home and the seller suggested that you pay for improvements up front without a clear agreement on reimbursement or price adjustment, would you think that's fair? I will guess not.

In this case, the trust should bear costs incurred to generate rental income for your mother's care, with any advance by one beneficiary treated explicitly as a reimbursable expense rather than a retroactive price reduction. And then sell the house on the open market if consensus cannot be reached on how to account for those costs. Otherwise, no one will feel like they're getting a fair deal, and it will turn into one of those family stories, steeped in bitterness and resentment, that will linger for years. In 10 years' time, when the house is worth $5 million, you will curse your sister for convincing you to sell this home to her.

There is a reason people bid on a home anonymously, through attorneys. It allows both parties to get the best price they believe they can afford or believe is fair without muddying the waters with personal obligations and emotional leverage. It's business. It's not always easy, but it's a lot easier to play hardball as a buyer or seller when you don't know who you're dealing with and have no personal relationship and when costs, repairs, and credits are spelled out in writing rather than negotiated informally after the fact.

Don't miss: My wife asked for a divorce after 21 years. She wants to buy a house with our savings, but promises to help pay my mortgage.

Everyone has an agenda

Reading the first few sentences of your letter, one thing was obvious: You and your sister would come a cropper on the subject of the fair market value of this house. It was as clear to me as the stars in the Hawaiian night sky. Everyone is trying to do everyone a favor, and everyone has an agenda (a sibling buys the house without a bidding war, obviously, and the house stays in the family). Add to that the trust's obligation to support your mother, and every dollar spent or forgiven takes on extra emotional weight.

You don't sell a $3 million property and, possibly, lose out on $200,000 or more of an inheritance - although there's no guarantee you would get that price; it's just speculation at this stage - because the buyer whined so loudly and persistently that you just gave in. That's not logical or financially astute or adult behavior. At the same time, you also don't allow a buyer to advance money for trust maintenance without clearly defining whether that payment is a loan, a gift, or a reimbursable expense. You sell a house at the price you believe will benefit all siblings, not just one sibling who has mastered the art of being a squeaky wheel.

In these kinds of situations where family and finance are mixed up, somebody always ends up feeling shortchanged, and no one will ever admit to being wrong. In addition to the financial transaction, you have years of healthy and unhealthy familial relationships and group dynamics messing with everyone's perspective. You're wrong. And so is your sister. You're right. And so is your sister. The conflict isn't really about $100,000; it's about unclear rules layered on top of a deliberately non-market deal. You've fallen into a trap that many families do when a parent dies.

You decided to make up your own rulebook, and you are all paying the price.

Related: I'm 70, a widow and struggled financially. How do I let go of the resentment I feel towards my wealthy, unhelpful brother?

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

My husband will inherit $180K. I think we should invest the money. He wants to pay off his $168K mortgage. Who's right?

'I'm at a loss': My boyfriend of nearly 10 years is naming his elderly parents as beneficiaries and giving them power of attorney. Am I right to be upset?

'We have no prenuptial agreement': Will my wife be able to take my money if I transfer it to my retirement account?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1718ET

data.title: Nearly 15,000 U.S. flights have been canceled due to the winter storm - and counting
data.description:
By Claudia Assis

American and Southwest flights going in and out of Dallas-Fort Worth were among the most affected Saturday

Dallas Fort Worth International Airport has been among the air-travel hubs most affected by this weekend's winter storm.

Airlines have canceled nearly 15,000 flights through Monday due to a winter storm expected to wallop two-thirds of the U.S. over the weekend - with flights into and out of the Dallas-Fort Worth area in Texas among the most affected so far.

The widespread air-travel disruptions come amid forecasts for heavy snow and frigid temperatures in the Northeast and Midwest, as well as icy conditions and gusty winds across the southern Great Plains and Southeast, the National Weather Service said.

Nearly 700 flights on Friday, more than 3,800 flights on Saturday, over 8,500 flights on Sunday and almost 1,500 flights on Monday had been canceled as of Saturday afternoon, according to FlightAware.

Saturday's cancellations most significantly affected flights in and out of Dallas Fort Worth International Airport, while the heaviest of Sunday's cancellations were at Charlotte Douglas International Airport in North Carolina and Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport.

Dallas Forth Worth and Charlotte Douglas are the two largest hubs for Fort Worth-based American Airlines (AAL), while Hartsfield-Jackson is Atlanta-based Delta Air Lines' (DAL) top hub. Cancellations also hit Dallas Love Field, home to Southwest Airlines' (LUV) corporate headquarters. American and Southwest were feeling the brunt of Saturday's disruptions, with nearly 1,000 and 600 cancellations, respectively.

Sunday cancellations were most heavily hitting American (over 1,300 flights), Delta (nearly 1,100 flights) and Southwest (nearly 1,000 flights), at last check, with the most affected airports being Charlotte Douglas and Hartsfield-Jackson. Major airports in the New York City, Washington, D.C., and Boston areas were also seeing heavy Sunday cancellations.

On an average day, about 1% of roughly 50,000 daily U.S. flights are canceled and some 20% are delayed. On Jan. 22, 2025, at the peak of a historic snowstorm that blanketed the Southeast, some 1,700 flights were canceled.

The single worst day for flight cancellations in recent years was July 19, 2024, which saw 3,600 cancellations when a faulty software update involving cybersecurity company CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD) rattled computers running Windows (MSFT) and the global information-technology system with the "blue screen of death," affecting airlines, airport operations and other businesses.

For the current storm, U.S. airlines have issued waivers to ticket holders, with the details of what is covered varying from airline to airline.

Once a flight is canceled, there's very little slack in the system for rebookings, as airlines work to fly planes as close to capacity as possible. That makes for slow rebooking and recovery from any flight cancellations.

The storm is expected to bring heavy snow, ice and bitter cold to large swaths of the country. It is shaping up to be particularly treacherous, as it is expected to hit areas not used to severe winter weather.

U.S. airline stocks finished in the red Friday, a mixed day broadly for the U.S. equity market. The U.S. Global Jets exchange-traded fund JETS ended 1.1% lower on the day.

-Claudia Assis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1625ET

data.title: Yes, 'DOGE' put our Social Security data at risk. Here's what lawmakers are doing about it.
data.description:
By Jessica Hall

Trump administration admits to security breaches that had been outlined in whistleblower complaint

A whistleblower had said in August that employees of the so-called Department of Government Efficiency had misused Social Security Administration information.

Two Democratic congressmen are calling for a criminal investigation into the entity known as the Department of Government Efficiency after the Trump administration admitted in court filings that the organization shared Social Security data on an unauthorized private server.

The Social Security Administration said in a Jan. 16 court filing that "DOGE" employees tried to hand over sensitive personal records to an unnamed advocacy group seeking to "overturn election results" and sent confidential information on about 1,000 Americans to Elon Musk's workers.

"The 'DOGE' appointees engaged in this scheme - who were never brought before Congress for approval or even publicly identified - must be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law for these abhorrent violations of the public trust," said House Social Security Subcommittee ranking member John Larson, a Connecticut Democrat, and Ways and Means Committee ranking member Richard Neal, a Democrat from Massachusetts, in a joint statement.

"Elon Musk and his 'DOGE' believe they are above the law and refused to appear before Congress. Republicans have blocked every effort to hold this administration accountable, voting down our resolutions demanding documents, even changing House Rules to shield 'DOGE' from accountability and protect themselves from having to take another vote on the issue," Larson and Neal said.

The call for a criminal investigation follows a court filing by the Social Security Administration outlining how DOGE workers used the third-party cloud service Cloudflare in March 2025 - which was against the agency's security policies.

"SSA did not know, until its recent review, that DOGE team members were using Cloudflare during this period. Because Cloudflare is a third-party entity, SSA has not been able to determine exactly what data were shared to Cloudflare or whether the data still exist on the server," according to the court filing.

The disclosure mirrors what Chuck Borges, the former chief data officer at the SSA, said in his whistleblower complaint, warning that the private data of more than 300 million Americans was at risk after DOGE employees uploaded a copy of the Social Security Administration's database to a cloud environment. Borges did not immediately respond to a MarketWatch request for comment.

Read: Your Social Security number is at risk, thanks to DOGE workers - along with every single SSN in America, whistleblower says

The Social Security Administration notified the Justice Department of its concerns on Dec. 10, 2025, and notified the court about a month later. The agency also said in the court filing that it referred the two DOGE employees to the Office of Special Counsel for a potential violation of the Hatch Act, a law that ensures that federal workers operate free of political influence or coercion.

Read: Social Security's 'DOGE' whistleblower is running for public office as the 'longest of long shots'

"SSA is entrusted with the sensitive data of hundreds of millions of Americans, and protecting that data from illegal use must be a top priority. Anyone involved must be held accountable and the Social Security Administration must take immediate steps to ensure nothing like this can happen again," said Nancy LeaMond, AARP's chief advocacy and engagement officer.

The disclosures in the court filing come after a tumultuous year for the Social Security Administration, which was overseen by the Musk-led DOGE in what was trumpeted as an effort to root out waste, fraud and abuse. The agency also saw several leadership changes, job cuts, regional office closures and troubles with its telephone customer service in the past year.

Read: Social Security's new commissioner, rule reversals and cost cuts: Here's everything that's happening at the agency

The Social Security Administration did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

"This week's revelations are just the tip of the iceberg. We need to know exactly who has our data and what they are doing with it. And those who have committed illegal acts must be prosecuted," said Alex Lawson, executive director of advocacy group Social Security Works.

From the archives (February 2025): Worried about Elon Musk and 'DOGE' getting your Social Security number? Here's how to protect yourself.

-Jessica Hall

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1537ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 67 million Americans could lose telehealth coverage in the coming days unless Congress approves funding
data.description:
By Jessica Hall

Telehealth has become a 'mainstream part of care delivery'

A new study from the University of Michigan shows telehealth does not result in runaway medical usage.

Telehealth services for Medicare beneficiaries which were slated to end later this month may get a two-year extension that could keep in place the pandemic-era services available to more than 67 million beneficiaries.

Under a funding package released by the U.S. House appropriations committee on Tuesday, Medicare's telehealth coverage would be extended until Dec. 31, 2027. Policymakers face a Jan. 30 deadline to extend telehealth funding, or coverage that has been in effect since March 2020 will lapse.

"We've seen before that even strong, bipartisan proposals can face unexpected hurdles late in the process, which is why it's important not to take any outcome for granted when it comes to congressional legislation," said Alexis Apple, deputy executive director of ATA Action and vice president of federal affairs at the American Telemedicine Association. "While this package does not provide a permanent solution, it represents a significant win for ... patients who rely on telehealth services across the country."

Telehealth services help bridge the gap for people for whom transportation or mobility is a problem and in rural areas where doctors may be unavailable. The common types of medical appointments carried out via telehealth range from mental-health visits to dermatological screenings to conversations with a primary-care doctor about cold or flu symptoms or routine concerns.

The telehealth coverage is part of a government funding proposal totaling roughly $1.2 trillion in spending for the departments of labor, health and human services, education, defense, transportation and other agencies. The government faces a shutdown deadline of Jan. 30.

The funding package also comes as new data shows that telehealth services do not result in overuse of medical services, which may help allay concerns about runaway usage and funding problems.

The new findings, published in Health Affairs Scholar by a team from the University of Michigan Institute for Healthcare Policy and Innovation, showed that overall healthcare visits have remained stable or declined over time.

According to the University of Michigan study, among non-surgical medical specialties and mental-health providers, the total number of visits stabilized and even declined slightly through June 2024, the most recent period available to analyze.

That study found that telehealth accounts for 44% of all behavioral health visits and 9% of primary-care visits among beneficiaries in traditional Medicare. The data reflects more than 60 million people who had nearly 539 million appointments during a five-year period.

Telehealth services boomed during the COVID-19 pandemic and handled about 42% of Medicare's outpatient visits during its height, according to Ateev Mehrotra, a professor at Brown University's School of Public Health. Volume then had fallen to about 5% as of 2024, according to Mehrotra.

This fall, telehealth coverage was disrupted and usage fell during the government shutdown, Mehrota and co-authors wrote in a new report. The usage rebounded when the government reopened, but any disruption to coverage could affect telehealth usage again.

"Telehealth is no longer a temporary solution; it has become a mainstream part of care delivery," Mehrota and the co-authors said. "Without congressional action to reinstate telemedicine flexibilities, both beneficiaries and providers will continue to face disruptions in access to care, loss of critical services and wasted investments in telehealth infrastructure."

Greater telehealth usage leads to fewer emergency-department visits and better medication management among patients, but there's still an overall cost increase for Medicare. A 2024 study in JAMA said the higher usage of telehealth services led to a higher cost of care of $164.99 per Medicare beneficiary.

Before the pandemic, telehealth was possible only under very limited circumstances in Medicare. If Congress does not extend the services, Medicare recipients in rural areas will still be able to use telehealth services, but only if they are accessing the technology from a medical building such as a doctor's office or a hospital to speak with a provider located elsewhere.

Read: 67 million Medicare recipients face 'chaos' if Congress cuts telehealth benefits

All Medicare recipients will also still be able to use telehealth for three conditions: stroke, mental illness and substance-abuse disorders.

-Jessica Hall

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1538ET

data.title: Small stocks have crushed big ones this year, but that may just be a mirage
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

Small-cap investors are convinced the stock-market tide has turned in their favor

Small-cap stocks are off to a strong start in 2026. But investors shouldn't get carried away just yet.

Small-cap's relative strength doesn't have longer-term significance because it is caused by short-term seasonal factors.

Small-cap stocks are having a great start to the year, but that doesn't mean their performance will get bigger from here.

It's understandable why long-suffering small-cap investors are so eager to declare that the tide has turned in their favor. Despite numerous past studies finding that small caps have significantly outperformed large caps over the past century, small stocks on average have suffered for some time.

Over the 20 years through the end of 2025, for example, the Russell Top 200 Index XX:RT200 of the 200 largest stocks in the Russell 1000 Index RUI beat the small-stock Russell 2000 RUT by 3.5 percentage points annualized. And it outperformed the Russell Microcap Index of the smallest of the small caps by 4.7 annualized percentage points.

But this year through Jan. 20, the Russell Top 200 has lost 1.3% versus a 6.6% gain for the Russell 2000 and 7.6% for the Russell Microcap Index.

Small caps' relative strength doesn't have longer-term significance because it is caused by short-term seasonal factors. One such factor is that tax-loss selling - investors selling losers to offset any capital gains - came to an end in December. That selling disproportionately impacted smaller stocks, which helps to explain why they have bounced back so strongly this year. Another short-term seasonal factor is that large institutional investors have an incentive in early January to shift some of their equity exposure away from the largest stocks and into the smallest issues (as I discussed in a column last fall).

To document that early-January small-cap returns don't have long-term significance, I calculated the Russell indices' returns over the first two weeks of each of the past 20 calendar years. Even though the larger-cap indices came out far ahead of the smaller-cap benchmarks over the entire 20-year period, the reverse was true for the first two weeks of January: On average over this two-week period, the Russell Microcap's return was higher than those of the Russell mid-cap and large-cap indices.

Are small stocks really undervalued?

Many small-cap investors also make a valuation-based argument for why they believe that small-cap relative strength so far this year portends something more sustainable. But there are flaws in this argument: On average small-cap stocks are more overvalued than the largest issues.

Consider how the price/earnings ratio is calculated for an index that contains companies that are losing money, which is the case with many smaller companies. Many index providers simply ignore these losing stocks, which can artificially lower the index's reported P/E ratio. When excluding companies with negative earnings, for example, the Russell 2000 index of small- and mid-cap stocks recently had a P/E ratio approaching 20.0 - well-below the S&P 500's SPX nearly 26.0 P/E ratio. But when losing companies are included in the calculation, as they should be, the Russell 2000's P/E ratio jumps to about 37.0.

The bottom line? Small-cap stocks may beat the large caps in 2026. But whether they do so has nothing to do with their performance this year so far.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

Also read: Tax-related selling could set up these 11 stocks for big gains early in 2026

More: These under-the-radar stocks combine fast growth with big upside potential

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1527ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: This stock-market indicator just flashed one of its most bullish signals since 2000
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

Market timers ran for the exits after Tuesday's big drop. That's a good sign.

Market timers' short-term panic is a positive signal for longer-term investors.

A hallmark of a major market top is the widespread belief that declines are buying opportunities.

The stock market's big dive on Tuesday most likely did not signal the end of the bull market. That's because investors typically react to major market tops by stubbornly refusing to believe the bull market is over - and that is not how market timers reacted to Tuesday's rout.

The average short-term timer that my firm tracks reduced recommended equity exposure on Tuesday by almost 20 percentage points, as judged by the Hulbert Stock Newsletter Sentiment Index. That's one of the biggest one-day HSNSI drops since 2000, which is how far back data extend.

Since many believe that the stock market is forming a bubble top analogous to the top of the internet bubble in March 2000, it's helpful to contrast the HSNSI's big drop on Tuesday with how it behaved in early 2000. The Nasdaq Composite Index COMP peaked on March 10, 2000, and over the following two weeks this benchmark dropped more than 10% on an intraday basis - enough to satisfy the semiofficial criterion for a stock-market correction. Yet over those two weeks, the HSNSI not only didn't fall, it rose 2.5 percentage points.

That's a revealing contrast. In the wake of a stock-market drop that was only one-fifth as big as in March 2000, the HSNSI this week fell by almost 20 percentage points rather than rising.

Contrarian analysis tells us that one hallmark of a major market top is the widespread belief that declines are buying opportunities. Confirmation comes from analyzing the "Buy-on-Dips Confidence Index" from Yale University finance professor Robert Shiller. The index represents the percentage of retail investors who believe that the stock market will rise the day after big drops. As you can see from the chart below, the S&P 500 SPX has performed far worse in the wake of high index levels than when the index is low.

Because Shiller updates the Buy-on-Dips Confidence Index with a several-month lag, we don't know where it stands currently. But it almost certainly is fairly low, given that drop of nearly 20 percentage points in the HSNSI on Tuesday.

Note that this discussion applies only to the stock market's short-term prospects. The market remains extremely overvalued, as I've written before, so it would not be surprising if the bull market tops out sooner rather than later. But if that top - whenever it occurs - follows historical norms, it will be met with widespread disbelief - just the opposite of how market timers reacted to Tuesday's big drop.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com.

More: Small stocks have crushed big ones this year, but that may just be a mirage

Also read: The history of geopolitical turmoil finds they usually lead to stock-market gains.

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-24-26 1527ET

data.title: REFUGEES SUE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION TO STOP ICE TERROR CAMPAIGN
data.description:
REFUGEES SUE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION TO STOP ICE TERROR CAMPAIGN

REFUGEES SUE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION TO STOP ICE TERROR CAMPAIGN

PR Newswire

MINNEAPOLIS, Jan. 24, 2026

Minnesota Refugees Face Unlawful Arrest, Detention, and Interrogation

MINNEAPOLIS, Jan. 24, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Last night, a group of refugees represented by the International Refugee Assistance Project (IRAP), Berger Montague, and the Center for Human Rights and Constitutional Law (CHRCL) filed a federal class action lawsuit challenging the illegal arrest and imprisonment of resettled refugees in Minnesota. Individual plaintiffs are lawfully resettled refugees who are not yet legal permanent residents. Many did apply for their green cards, but have been unable to receive them due to the Trump administration's suspension of processing for refugees' applications and long-standing processing delays. The Advocates for Human Rights, a non-profit providing on-the-ground legal representation to impacted refugees in Minnesota, is an organizational plaintiff. 

Since the announcement of Operation PARRIS on January 9, armed Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents are going door to door arresting members of the Minnesota refugee community, including children and the elderly, and spiriting them off to detention camps in Texas. Refugees who assert their legal right to refuse ICE entry into their homes without a judicial warrant are then receiving letters calling them to appear at a local ICE office, where they are immediately detained without explanation, probable cause, or due process. Others have had their cars followed or been picked up on their way to work or school, making many refugee community members terrified to even leave the house. 

"I fled my home country because I was facing government repression," said D. Doe, plaintiff. "I can't believe it's happening again here. It's chilling and I'm scared."

Once jailed in Texas, refugees are frequently put through intensive questioning without adequate opportunity to prepare or consult with a lawyer. Many have been forced to answer the same questions they were already asked when they initially proved to the U.S. government they were refugees or on their green card applications, this time without the opportunity to review the paperwork they may have submitted months or years ago. Those ICE has released were left on the streets of Texas, usually without money, identification, or any way to get home. Other refugees have been forced to stay in detention in Texas for a week or more, with no information about why they are being detained or when they will be released. 

"Refugees are being stripped from their families, carried across state borders, and held in inhumane detention centers guarded by armed agents, high fences, and barbed wire," said Taqwa Elhindi, a Supervising Attorney at IRAP representing detained refugees in Texas. "One elderly woman and her son were taken to facilities in separate cities, unaware of the others' whereabouts. In addition to being separated from their loved ones, refugees face immense barriers trying to contact their lawyers, leaving them alone and confused about why they are being held when they have adhered to every requirement imposed by the U.S. government." 

The lawsuit argues that the U.S. government has no legal authority to detain lawfully present refugees without charge, and seeks to halt the U.S. government's illegal, discriminatory, and cruel practice of warrantless arrest, unauthorized detention, and coercive interrogation.

"ICE is hunting down refugees and disappearing them from their homes," said Kimberly Grano, Staff Attorney, U.S. Litigation at IRAP. "The Trump administration is intentionally and illegally terrorizing resettled refugees who are not accused of any wrongdoing. Refugees are being re-traumatized, families are being separated, and entire communities are living in fear. It is clear the administration intends to use these baseless detentions and coercive interviews to intimidate refugees and try to terminate the status of people this country promised to protect. We are suing to end these illegal tactics immediately."

"ICE agents are dragging refugees from their homes in Minnesota, including children and families, flying them to brutal prisons in Texas, and attempting to strip them of legal protections," said Sarah Kahn, a Senior Staff Attorney at CHRCL. "They target them based on their home country and where they live without any reason to believe they have done anything wrong. There is no reason for this fascist, violent persecution of refugees except hatred for immigrants."

"The victimization of refugees as part of a political payback is abhorrent. Refugees wait years to come to the United States, often to escape totalitarian regimes," said Berger Montague Executive Shareholder E. Michelle Drake. "ICE is now behaving worse than the dictators from whom many of our clients escaped. ICE's conduct is shameful and illegal, and we look forward to achieving justice in court."

"The federal government is tearing refugees from their homes and communities, shattering their hard-won safety and security," said Michele Garnett McKenzie, Executive Director of The Advocates for Human Rights. "DHS is inflicting this cruelty without providing legal justification or an opportunity for refugees to access their attorneys and defend themselves from imprisonment. This attack on refugees is directly contrary to The Advocates object and purpose: ensuring all Governments, especially that of the United States, respect essential legal protections enshrined in the International Refugee Convention. Operation PARRIS has also forced The Advocates for Human Rights to divert scarce resources and time to opposing this illegal operation. International and U.S. law are clear – we have a legal and moral obligation to protect individuals who face persecution, not expose them to imprisonment, isolation, coercion, and abuse. The government must cease these illegal actions that undermine the commitment we made to these refugees, causing grave harm to individuals, families, and communities."

Additional Information

  • Read the complaint: HERE
  • Read IRAP's statement condemning the arrest and detention of refugees: HERE
  • Access Know Your Rights information for refugees living in the United States: HERE

The International Refugee Assistance Project (IRAP) is a global legal aid and advocacy organization working to create a world where refugees and all people seeking safety are empowered to claim their right to freedom of movement and a path to lasting refuge. Everyone should have a safe place to live and a safe way to get there.
www.refugeerights.org

The Advocates for Human Rights is an independent, nonprofit, nongovernmental human rights organization based in Minneapolis, Minnesota. The Advocates for Human Rights works in our home community and around the world to ensure access to immigration justice, end violence against women, abolish the death penalty, and protect the rights of LGBTIQ+ people. We are the Upper Midwest's primary provider of free immigration legal services for people fleeing persecution, torture, and violence.
www.theadvocatesforhumanrights.org 

The Center for Human Rights and Constitutional Law (CHRCL) is a legal non-profit committed to protecting and advancing the rights of immigrants through legal action, advocacy, and education. Through impact litigation, we challenge unlawful immigration policies to drive systemic change and establish stronger legal protections for immigrants. At the local, state, and federal levels, we advocate for fair and humane policies that uphold the rights of all immigrants.
www.centerforhumanrights.org

Berger Montague is one of the nation's preeminent law firms focusing on complex civil litigation, class actions, and mass torts in federal and state courts throughout the United States. With more than $2.4 billion in 2025 post-trial judgments alone, the Firm is a leader in the fields of complex litigation, antitrust, consumer protection, defective products, environmental law, employment law, securities, and whistleblower cases, among many other practice areas. For over 55 years, Berger Montague has played leading roles in precedent-setting cases and has recovered over $50 billion for its clients and the classes they have represented. Berger Montague is headquartered in Philadelphia and has offices in Chicago; Malvern, PA; Minneapolis; San Diego; San Francisco; Toronto, Canada; Washington, D.C., and Wilmington, DE.

MEDIA CONTACTS
IRAP | Spencer Tilger | media@refugeerights.org
The Advocates | Madeline Lohman | press@advrights.org
CHRCL | Sergio Perez | press@centerforhumanrights.org
Berger Montague | Amy Wall-Monte | awallmonte@bergermontague.com

data.title: Gold IRA Rollover Guide 2026 Released (Fees, Kit, Instructions and More)
data.description:
IRAEmpire has published a new and updated guide on gold IRA rollovers to help consumers understand this process and avoid mistakes.

FORT LAUDERDALE, FLORIDA / ACCESS Newswire / January 24, 2026 / As economic uncertainty, inflation concerns, and market volatility continue to shape financial planning decisions, many investors are exploring alternative ways to protect their retirement savings. One increasingly popular strategy is a Gold IRA rollover, which allows individuals to move funds from existing retirement accounts into a precious metals-backed IRA.

Overview of Best Gold IRA Rollover Companies 2026:

  1. Augusta Precious Metals (Best for Large Investments)

  2. Lear Capital (Best Customer Support)

  3. Goldencrest Metals (Best Prices)

  4. Preserve Gold (Best Products

A Gold IRA rollover offers exposure to physical gold while maintaining the tax advantages of a traditional retirement account. This approach appeals to investors who want diversification beyond stocks and bonds and are looking for long-term wealth preservation strategies.

However, the rollover process involves specific IRS rules, timelines, and compliance requirements that must be followed to avoid penalties.

According to Michael Hunt, Senior Writer at IRAEmpire, "Understanding how Gold IRA rollovers work, which accounts are eligible, and what steps are required is essential before making any decisions."

>>Find The Best Gold Investment Company of Your State Here.

What Is a Gold IRA Rollover?

A Gold IRA rollover is the process of moving funds from an existing retirement account into a self-directed Individual Retirement Account (IRA) that is designed to hold physical precious metals, such as gold. This allows investors to maintain the tax-advantaged status of their retirement savings while gaining exposure to an alternative asset.

Check Out The Full Free Gold IRA Rollover Guide

In a rollover, retirement funds are distributed from the original account and then deposited into the new Gold IRA within a specified time frame. This process is commonly used when investors want to change the type of assets held in their retirement account without triggering immediate taxes or penalties.

It's important to distinguish a rollover from a transfer. A rollover involves the movement of funds through the account holder, while a transfer typically moves funds directly between custodians. Understanding this distinction is critical, as different IRS rules apply to each method.

Gold IRA rollovers are often used when transitioning from employer-sponsored plans or when investors want greater control over how their retirement funds are allocated.

Why Investors Are Rolling Over Retirement Accounts Into Gold IRAs

Many investors consider rolling over retirement accounts into Gold IRAs as part of a broader effort to manage risk and preserve long-term purchasing power. Traditional retirement portfolios are often heavily weighted toward stocks and bonds, which can be sensitive to inflation, interest rate changes, and market volatility.

Hunt highlights, "Gold has historically been viewed as a hedge during periods of economic uncertainty. By including physical gold in a retirement account, investors aim to diversify their holdings and reduce reliance on paper-based assets. This diversification can help balance portfolio performance during market downturns."

Another reason investors pursue Gold IRA rollovers is concern over currency depreciation and rising government debt. As monetary policy evolves, some investors look to tangible assets that are not directly tied to the performance of fiat currencies.

Additionally, Gold IRAs offer a structured and regulated way to hold physical gold within a retirement framework. This combination of diversification, asset security, and tax-advantaged treatment explains why Gold IRA rollovers continue to attract attention in 2026.

Learn About the Best Gold IRA Rollover Providers in the US

Eligible Retirement Accounts for a Gold IRA Rollover

Not all retirement accounts are treated the same when it comes to Gold IRA rollovers. Understanding which accounts are eligible helps investors plan the rollover process correctly and avoid unnecessary tax complications.

Traditional IRAs are commonly eligible for rollovers into a Gold IRA. Since these accounts are already tax-deferred, moving funds into a self-directed Gold IRA can typically be done without triggering immediate taxes, provided IRS rules are followed.

Employer-sponsored plans such as 401(k)s may also be eligible, particularly if the account is associated with a former employer. Some active 401(k) plans allow in-service rollovers, though this depends on the specific plan rules set by the employer.

Access The Full Gold IRA Rollover Guide Here

Other retirement accounts that may qualify include 403(b) plans, 457 plans, and the Thrift Savings Plan (TSP). Each account type has its own guidelines, making it important to review plan documentation or consult a qualified professional before initiating a rollover.

By confirming eligibility in advance, investors can ensure a smoother transition into a Gold IRA and maintain compliance throughout the process.

Gold IRA Rollover Rules and IRS Guidelines (2026 Update)

Gold IRA rollovers are governed by specific IRS rules designed to preserve the tax-advantaged status of retirement accounts. One of the most important regulations is the 60-day rollover rule. Once funds are distributed from the original retirement account, they must be deposited into the new Gold IRA within 60 days. Missing this deadline can result in taxes and potential early withdrawal penalties.

Another key guideline is the one-rollover-per-year rule, which applies to IRA-to-IRA rollovers. This rule limits investors to one rollover within a 12-month period, regardless of how many IRA accounts they own. Direct transfers between custodians are not subject to this limitation.

The IRS also sets strict requirements on the type of gold that can be held in a Gold IRA. Only approved gold coins and bars that meet purity standards are allowed, and the metals must be stored in an IRS-approved depository. Personal possession of the gold is not permitted.

Staying compliant with these rules is essential. Even minor mistakes in timing, asset selection, or storage can lead to unintended tax consequences, making careful planning especially important in 2026.

Check which Gold IRA Rollover Company is Best For You

Step-by-Step Gold IRA Rollover Process

Completing a Gold IRA rollover involves several structured steps designed to ensure compliance and protect the tax-advantaged status of retirement funds. Understanding each stage helps investors avoid delays and costly mistakes.

The first step is choosing a qualified self-directed IRA custodian that supports precious metals. The custodian is responsible for administering the account, handling paperwork, and ensuring IRS compliance.

Next, the investor opens a self-directed Gold IRA with the chosen custodian. Once the account is established, funds are moved from the existing retirement account through a rollover or transfer, depending on eligibility and preference.

After funding the account, the investor selects IRS-approved gold products. These may include specific coins or bars that meet purity requirements. The custodian facilitates the purchase on the investor's behalf.

Finally, the gold is shipped to and stored in an IRS-approved depository. Secure storage is mandatory and ensures compliance with retirement account regulations.

By following this step-by-step process, investors can complete a Gold IRA rollover in an organized and compliant manner.

Best Gold IRA Rollover Companies 2026

Choosing the right provider is one of the most important steps in completing a Gold IRA rollover. The best Gold IRA rollover companies are typically distinguished by their experience, transparency, customer education, and ability to guide investors through IRS-compliant rollover processes. Based on the rankings used previously, the following companies are frequently recognized by investors researching Gold IRA rollovers.

Augusta Precious Metals is commonly ranked at the top due to its education-first approach and strong focus on compliance. Investors often highlight structured guidance around rollovers, clear explanations of IRS rules, and a consultative onboarding process. This makes Augusta a popular choice for investors prioritizing long-term retirement planning over short-term transactions.

Lear Capital is another well-known Gold IRA rollover provider with a long operating history in the precious metals space. The company is often noted for its broad selection of eligible metals and its experience handling rollovers from various retirement accounts, including 401(k)s and traditional IRAs.

Goldencrest Metals has gained attention for its personalized service model. Investors researching rollovers often cite one-on-one support and simplified processes as key strengths, particularly for those navigating a Gold IRA rollover for the first time.

Preserve Gold rounds out the list, frequently mentioned for its focus on asset preservation and retirement diversification. The company is often evaluated favorably by investors seeking a straightforward rollover process combined with secure storage options.

Ultimately, the best Gold IRA rollover company depends on individual priorities, but these providers consistently appear in investor research due to their experience, reputation, and rollover support.

>>Find The Best Gold IRA Rollover Company of Your State Here.

Direct Rollover vs Indirect Rollover - What's the Difference?

When completing a Gold IRA rollover, investors typically choose between a direct rollover and an indirect rollover. Understanding the difference between these two methods is critical, as each carries different risks and responsibilities.

A direct rollover involves moving funds directly from the existing retirement account to the new Gold IRA custodian. The investor never takes possession of the money, which reduces the risk of missing deadlines or triggering taxes. Because of its simplicity and lower compliance risk, direct rollovers are generally preferred by most investors.

An indirect rollover, on the other hand, involves the distribution of funds to the account holder first. The investor must then redeposit the funds into the Gold IRA within 60 days. While this method is allowed, it carries greater risk. Missing the deadline can result in taxes and early withdrawal penalties, and mandatory withholding may apply.

Due to these factors, many investors choose direct rollovers when possible. This approach offers a smoother process and helps minimize the chance of costly mistakes during the transition.

What Type of Gold Can You Hold in a Gold IRA?

The IRS imposes strict guidelines on the types of gold that are permitted in a Gold IRA. These rules are designed to ensure that retirement accounts hold investment-grade precious metals rather than collectibles or speculative items.

To be eligible, gold must meet specific purity requirements, generally a minimum of 99.5% purity. Commonly approved products include certain gold bars and widely recognized bullion coins that satisfy these standards. Examples often include government-minted coins and bars produced by accredited refiners.

Not all gold products qualify. Collectible coins, rare coins, and jewelry are typically not allowed in a Gold IRA, even if they are made of gold. Holding non-approved items can result in the entire account being treated as a distribution, leading to taxes and penalties.

Additionally, all gold held within a Gold IRA must be stored in an IRS-approved depository. Personal storage or home safes are not permitted. Understanding these requirements helps investors select compliant assets and maintain the tax-advantaged status of their Gold IRA.

Costs and Fees Associated With a Gold IRA Rollover

A Gold IRA rollover involves several types of fees that investors should understand before moving retirement funds. While these costs are common across most self-directed precious metals IRAs, they can vary depending on the custodian, depository, and service structure.

One of the primary expenses is the custodial fee, which covers account setup, administration, recordkeeping, and IRS reporting. Some custodians charge a one-time setup fee, while others apply annual maintenance fees.

Another key cost is storage and insurance. Because IRS rules require gold to be held in an approved depository, investors must pay for secure storage and insurance coverage. Fees may differ based on whether storage is segregated or non-segregated and the overall value of the metals stored.

There may also be transaction-related costs, including dealer markups or spreads when purchasing IRS-approved gold. These are typically reflected in the price paid above the spot price of gold.

Understanding these fees in advance helps investors evaluate the long-term cost of a Gold IRA rollover and determine whether it aligns with their retirement strategy.

Learn More About Gold IRA Rollover Here

Pros and Cons of a Gold IRA Rollover

A Gold IRA rollover can offer meaningful benefits, but it also comes with certain limitations that investors should consider carefully. Evaluating both sides helps determine whether this strategy fits within a broader retirement plan.

One of the main advantages is diversification. Adding physical gold to a retirement portfolio can reduce reliance on traditional financial assets and help manage risk during periods of market volatility. Gold IRAs also allow investors to hold a tangible asset while maintaining the tax-advantaged structure of an IRA.

Another benefit is long-term wealth preservation. Gold has historically been viewed as a hedge against inflation and currency depreciation, making it appealing to investors focused on protecting purchasing power over time.

However, there are also drawbacks. Gold IRAs involve additional fees for custody, storage, and administration, which can impact overall returns. Gold also does not generate income like dividends or interest, meaning returns depend primarily on price appreciation.

Because of these factors, a Gold IRA rollover may be more suitable for investors seeking diversification and stability rather than short-term growth.

Common Gold IRA Rollover Mistakes to Avoid

While a Gold IRA rollover can be an effective diversification strategy, mistakes during the process can lead to unnecessary taxes, penalties, or compliance issues. Being aware of common errors helps investors navigate the rollover more confidently.

One frequent mistake is missing the 60-day rollover deadline. If funds are not deposited into the new Gold IRA within the required timeframe, the IRS may treat the amount as a taxable distribution, potentially triggering early withdrawal penalties.

Another issue is selecting non-approved gold products. Purchasing coins or bars that do not meet IRS purity or eligibility standards can cause the entire account to lose its tax-advantaged status. It is essential to verify that all metals are IRS-approved before completing a purchase.

Some investors also overlook fees associated with custodians, storage, and transactions. Failing to understand the long-term cost structure can impact overall retirement outcomes.

Lastly, attempting to store gold personally rather than using an approved depository is a serious compliance violation. Avoiding these mistakes helps ensure a smoother and more secure Gold IRA rollover experience.

Be Sure to Only Work with The Best and Most Reliable Providers

Is a Gold IRA Rollover Right for You in 2026?

Whether a Gold IRA rollover is the right choice in 2026 depends on an investor's financial goals, risk tolerance, and long-term retirement strategy. Gold IRAs are often best suited for individuals who want to diversify away from traditional assets and add a tangible store of value to their retirement portfolio.

Investors concerned about inflation, market volatility, or overexposure to equities may find a Gold IRA rollover appealing. The structured nature of these accounts allows for physical gold ownership while maintaining tax advantages, which can be particularly attractive for long-term planners.

However, Gold IRAs are not ideal for every investor. The additional fees, lack of income generation, and strict compliance requirements mean they should be considered as part of a balanced portfolio rather than a standalone solution.

In 2026, informed decision-making is more important than ever. Evaluating personal financial objectives, understanding rollover rules, and considering professional guidance can help determine whether a Gold IRA rollover aligns with your retirement goals.

About IRAEmpire.com

IRAEmpire.com provides unbiased research, rankings, and educational resources to help Americans make informed decisions about Gold IRAs, precious metals, and retirement planning. Our mission is to offer transparent, data-driven guidance so investors can confidently protect and diversify their wealth with trusted gold investment companies across the United States.

CONTACT:
Ryan Paulson
ryan@iraempire.com

SOURCE: IRAEmpire LLC

data.title: I'm inheriting $250K. Will paying off my student loans and credit-card debt hurt my credit score?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'The money is coming from the proceeds of the sale of a family home'

"I want to pay off my student loans and credit-card debt." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I am about to inherit $250,000. The money is coming from the proceeds of the sale of a family home. I want to pay off my student loans and credit-card debt. If I wipe out all my students and credit-card debt, will that negatively affect my score?

Clearing My Debts

Related: 2025 has been one hell of a year. Consumers should expect more 'silent pain' in 2026.

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Your credit score could take a couple of months to recover, which won't impact your life unless you need to take out a car loan or you're signing for a mortgage during that period.

Dear Clearing,

Pay it all off. Have a ball.

Crack open a bottle of champagne (or prosecco). Experience life debt-free. Don't let a temporary hit to your credit score stop you from enjoying this moment. It's one giant step for you and a microscopic dent in mankind's debt mountain. Americans have more than $1.2 trillion in credit-card debt and $1.8 trillion in student-loan debt. Yours is an opportunity that millions of people with credit-card debt would love to have.

Your credit score could take a couple of months to recover, which won't impact your life unless you need to take out a car loan or you're signing for a mortgage during that period. "Your credit score will typically improve in one to two months," Experian says. "Lenders usually report your payment information to the national consumer credit bureaus (Experian, TransUnion and Equifax) after your billing cycle ends."

The same is true for installment loans like student debt. "The account was your only installment loan," Experian (EXPGY) adds. "Having a mix of revolving and installment credit helps your credit score. It was your only account with a low balance. Your score might dip if your remaining active accounts are a long way from being paid off. Any dips are usually temporary, though: Your credit score should go back to where it was within one or two months, all else being equal."

Assuming that your debts are broadly in line with the average credit-card debt ($6,000) and student-loan debt ($40,000), you will have a lot of money to invest/save.

A 40-something portfolio mix

If you were in your 40s, a moderate approach to investing would involve you putting 60%-75% in stocks and 25%-40% in bonds and cash equivalents. For a $200,000 portfolio with a moderate growth goal, you could split your funds into U.S. total market index funds (40%-45%, like an S&P 500 fund; favoring tech and small-cap if you wanted to lean into more growth potential), international stocks (15%-20%; with perhaps 5% of those in emerging markets).

The elephant in the room: How did you get into a situation where you were carrying unsustainable credit-card debt, and how can you make sure that it doesn't happen again? (Note: It's not always a person's fault when they rack up credit-card debt; job loss and other issues can contribute And while it's important to take accountability, doing so does not need to be accompanied by guilt-tripping or shaming people; that won't help anyone).

Consider the services of the National Foundation for Credit Counseling, a nonprofit that can help you put together a budget and a realistic plan to pay off your debt; a credit union, if you're a member of one; or the American Consumer Credit Counseling, another nonprofit that can help people who are in your position. Plus, 12-step support groups, including Debtors Anonymous, provide a safe space to talk about your background and your emotional life.

Don't leave this money hanging around in a checking account for too long. Given that inflation is hovering at just below 3%, the rising cost of living is snapping at your heels, at all our heels, all the time.

Don't miss: 'It's my money': My $800K inheritance is paying for a $1.6 million house. Shouldn't I decide where my husband and I live?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

'The house has quadrupled in value': I bought a house with my brother, but he did not contribute. How do I fix this?

My sister is buying our parents' $3 million house, but wants to deduct $100K for renovations. Who's right?

'I'm simply exhausted': I'm 55 and have $1.3 million for retirement. Can I retire next year?

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: 'People are often unreasonable when money is involved': My husband, 62, gave me a 5-year life estate. Would I have to pay for a new roof?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'He has included a provision stating that I would be responsible for "maintenance and expenses" during my occupancy'

"I understand that I should pay property taxes, insurance and routine upkeep of the house and yard." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

I live with my partner in his house, which has no mortgage. I sold my house when I moved in with him. He has drawn up a trust that would allow me to live in the house for five years if he predeceases me. After, he wants the house sold and proceeds split among his three children. I am 67 and he is 62. His children are 37, 30 and 28 years old. He has included a provision stating that I would be responsible for "maintenance and expenses" during my occupancy.

I understand that I should pay property taxes, insurance and routine upkeep of the house and yard. However, I am unclear about how major expenses should be handled. Is it reasonable for me to pay for a new roof, a new heating and cooling system, or other large capital expenses, given that I would only be living there for five years or less?

For example, if the furnace fails in the fourth year of my occupancy, does it make sense for me to pay for a new one if I would potentially have to leave the house a year later? My partner has said that his children would be reasonable. Based on my experience observing other families, I am concerned that people are often unreasonable when money is involved.

Do you have thoughts on how best to handle this? Should there be a clear agreement about large expenses, or do you think I should simply pay for whatever needs to be fixed during my potential five-year occupancy?

Wife & Stepmother

Related: When my friend was on her deathbed, her parents took $25,000 in cash and won't give it to her son. Do we tell him?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

When a spouse dies, there are a rake of financial considerations that fall upon the shoulders of the widow/widower.

Dear Wife,

The stepchildren's feelings and behavior remain the great unknown question mark hanging over your head, as your husband rightly alluded to.

Homeowners who fall in love should maintain their financial independence. Your husband's three children could, it's true, make it difficult for you if the life estate is not suitably specific. Any capital costs should, as a standard procedure, be paid by the remaindermen - in this case, your husband's children who will ultimately inherit the property - and they should be spelled out in the legal document. Other costs like hiring a painter to freshen up the kitchen or fixing the shower head would be under your purview.

The obvious solution is to clarify the terms in the trust or life estate regarding capital expenses, which might vary from year one to year five. Given that your husband fixed upon a five-year timeline as opposed to the rest of your life, it might be his way of acknowledging the unspoken (or not) feelings of his three children on the matter. A simple clause in the trust specifying that the "life tenant should pay routine upkeep, taxes and insurance," while the "remainder beneficiaries pay for capital improvements" would go some way in preventing a stalemate.

The situation, as you describe it, is a little more complicated, given that you will have five years to live in his home rent-free, except for property taxes, utilities and costs associated with other upkeep. Five years is quite an arbitrary length of time. You could, after all, be in your 70s or even 80s when you would have to move at the expiration of that time period. You would face a thousand small decisions about investing changes in the property while you're living there - everything from buying plants for the garden and fixing appliances to installing new carpet.

Amber flag

The fact that you sold your home when you married your husband raises an amber flag and, if you still had your house, you would have to worry less about where you would live if your husband predeceased you and more about how you would live. When a spouse dies, there are a rake of financial considerations that fall upon the shoulders of the widow/widower, including Social Security survivor benefits, tax implications for possibly withdrawing a higher amount from a 401(k) or IRA, and managing household expenses.

You have, I hope, held onto the proceeds from the sale of your home. You may need them, even if your husband does not pass away before you. You're both in your 60s and, with the age difference between you and your husband, it's not a given that he would die first (although men do tend to die earlier than women). One of the biggest determinants of your health as you age are your parents; the other would be keeping a close eye on your annual checkups, and making sure that you don't allow any worrying symptoms to persist without seeing a doctor.

But if your husband does die before you and you live in this house for another five years, you may feel an ill wind of discontent from any one of his three children who, depending on their financial circumstances, may resent you for holding up their inheritance. You would, to put it rather bluntly, be living inside a giant ATM machine with their names on it. Without your husband's support and protection, it may not feel like the same house, and I would not be surprised if you did not feel as comfortable occupying the property.

My tone may sound alarmist, but it's my attempt to be realistic, especially based on the number of letters I receive from and about stepmothers in this column. As for the proceeds from the sale of your home, you should keep enough of a cash cushion (12 months to two years) for any unexpected house or health expenses (replacing expensive goods and/or your own car). Make sure that your money is not sitting in a checking account earning zero interest, but rather in high-yield savings accounts, money market accounts and/or short-term CDs.

Moving in your 70s or 80s would require significant upheaval. It would be wise to envision that new life now. Planning your future is an emotional process as well as a financial one.

Related: I'm 70, a widow and struggled financially. How do I let go of the resentment I feel towards my wealthy, unhelpful brother?

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

My husband will inherit $180K. I think we should invest the money. He wants to pay off his $168K mortgage. Who's right?

'I'm at a loss': My boyfriend of nearly 10 years is naming his elderly parents as beneficiaries and giving them power of attorney. Am I right to be upset?

'We have no prenuptial agreement': Will my wife be able to take my money if I transfer it to my retirement account?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell
